meta.ui_subtitle: Theory exam · rivers, canals and lakes
meta.ui_demo: Full practice bank (40 questions, the exam format). Questions derived from freely-reusable French law (the inland-navigation police regulation, the 28 Sept. 2007 order). Not an official exam.
meta.ui_sourcenote: Source: French law (official act, freely reusable under the Open Licence / Etalab) — RIPAM, RGP, the 28 September 2007 order, Division 240. No question taken from an operator's bank.

questions.stem: Under the inland navigation police regulation (RGP), what is a “small craft” (menue embarcation)?

choices.text: A vessel under 20 metres
Any vessel under 24 metres
A vessel under 15 metres only

provenance.ref: RGP (Code des transports, art. R.4000-2, 7°) — menue embarcation (< 20 m)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: As a rule, a vessel with a hull length under 20 metres (excluding passenger vessels, tugs, etc.).

questions.stem: Where is mooring (stopping) of a vessel generally prohibited on a waterway?

choices.text: Under bridges and in narrow passages
Along every fitted quay
Nowhere — mooring is unrestricted

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-54-2 — stationnement interdit (ponts, passages étroits)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Mooring is forbidden where it hampers navigation (under bridges, in narrow passages) and wherever a sign prohibits it.

questions.stem: To limit wash (the wake that erodes banks and rocks moored boats), what must the driver do near the banks?

choices.text: Reduce speed
Increase speed to pass quickly
Hug the bank as close as possible

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-21 — vitesse et batillage
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Reduce speed when approaching banks, harbours and moored boats, to limit the wash.

questions.stem: At a lock entrance, what does a RED light mean?

choices.text: Entry forbidden, you must wait
Entry authorised
Exit from the lock only

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-31 §1 — feux d'écluse (rouge = accès interdit)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: A single red light means “no entry”; wait. A green light authorises entry; two red lights mean the lock is out of service.

questions.stem: Which light authorises entry into a lock?

choices.text: The green light
The red light
Two red lights

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-31 §1 — feu vert d'écluse (accès autorisé)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: A green light authorises entry. Two red lights mean the lock is out of service.

questions.stem: In a lock chamber, how should you handle your lines during locking?

choices.text: Tend and adjust them, never made fast to a fixed point
Make them fast hard to a fixed cleat
Remove all the lines

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-30/-31 — conduite dans le sas d'écluse
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The water level changes: tend the lines and pay out or take in as needed, never make them fast to a fixed point (risk of parting or listing).

questions.stem: On a waterway, what does a rectangular sign with a red border and a white horizontal bar mean?

choices.text: No passage / no entry
Obligation to stop and refuel
Recommendation to slow down

provenance.ref: RGP, annexe 5, panneau A.1 — interdiction de passer
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: It is a prohibition sign (type A.1): no entry / passage forbidden.

questions.stem: On an inland waterway, what does ONE prolonged blast from a vessel generally mean?

choices.text: “Attention”
“I am turning around”
“I am stopped”

provenance.ref: CEVNI — signal général « Attention » (un son prolongé)
provenance.source: CEVNI — Code européen des voies de navigation intérieure (CEE-ONU, Résolution n° 24)
provenance.licence: CEE-ONU — code européen modèle, librement consultable.

questions.explanation: One prolonged blast is the “Attention” signal; it draws other vessels' attention.

questions.stem: At night, a power-driven vessel underway on inland waters shows a light to port. What colour is it?

choices.text: Red
Green
Yellow

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-48-13 — feux des menues embarcations motorisées (bâbord rouge)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: As at sea: port sidelight red, starboard green, plus a white masthead light and a sternlight.

questions.stem: At night, a vessel moored away from the bank, outside a harbour, most often shows:

choices.text: An all-round white light
A flashing red light
No light

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-48-20 — signalisation en stationnement (feu blanc)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: An ordinary white light visible all round, to signal its presence to other vessels.

questions.stem: On inland waters, with respect to large vessels, ferries and vessels on a regular service, small craft (pleasure boats) must:

choices.text: Keep out of their way
Hold their course, having priority
Force them to stop

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-3 — menues embarcations s'écartent des grands bateaux et bacs
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Small craft must leave the way clear for large vessels, ferries and convoys: they keep out of their way.

questions.stem: As a general rule on an inland waterway, to which side should a vessel keep?

choices.text: To its right (starboard)
To its left (port)
In the middle of the channel

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-6 §1 — venir sur tribord (croisement bâbord-bâbord)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Each vessel keeps to its right (starboard) as a rule; vessels then pass port to port, unless signs indicate otherwise.

questions.stem: In a narrow passage (under a bridge, a tight channel) where crossing is impossible, what must the driver do?

choices.text: Make sure the passage is clear before entering
Always enter first
Speed up to force through

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-8 — passage étroit (priorité à l'avalant)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Make sure the passage is clear before entering and give way per the signs; on rivers priority goes to the down-bound vessel (going with the current).

questions.stem: On inland waters, what is a “down-bound” (avalant) vessel?

choices.text: A vessel going downstream
A vessel going upstream
A vessel stopped against the bank

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-1 et -53-6 §2 — montant/avalant (priorité à l'avalant)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: A down-bound vessel goes with the current (downstream); an up-bound (montant) one goes against it. On rivers the down-bound vessel, less manoeuvrable, has priority.

questions.stem: Along a river or canal, how is your position located?

choices.text: By kilometre points (bank markers)
By latitude and longitude alone
By nautical miles

provenance.ref: Voie navigable — repérage par points kilométriques (PK)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: By kilometre points (PK), shown by markers or plates on the banks, counting the kilometres from the start of the waterway.

questions.stem: Before passing under a bridge or a power line, what must the driver check?

choices.text: That the air draft is less than the available headroom
That its speed is at maximum
That its anchor is down

provenance.ref: RGP, annexe 5, panneau C.2 — hauteur libre limitée (tirant d'air)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: That the air draft (the boat's height above the water) is less than the available headroom, shown by the signs.

questions.stem: At a lock, who sets the order and manner in which vessels enter?

choices.text: The lock-keeper and the lock's signs
The fastest vessel
Each driver, freely

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-31 §4 et §12 — instructions de l'éclusier
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The lock-keeper (or the lock's signs / automatic system); follow their instructions and the lights.

questions.stem: At a lock, what do TWO red lights mean?

choices.text: The lock is out of service
Entry is immediately authorised
Exit is compulsory

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-31 §1(a) — deux feux rouges (écluse hors service)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The lock is out of service / closed to navigation. A single red light means “wait”, and a green light authorises entry.

questions.stem: On inland waters, how do you recognise a PROHIBITION sign?

choices.text: By its red border
By being entirely blue
By a green triangular shape

provenance.ref: RGP, annexe 5, section A — signaux d'interdiction (bord rouge)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Prohibition signs have a red border (often a white bar with red), like the “no entry” sign.

questions.stem: What does a rectangular BLUE sign with a white symbol generally give?

choices.text: Information / guidance
An absolute prohibition
An immediate danger

provenance.ref: RGP, annexe 5, section E — signaux d'indication (bleus)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Blue rectangular signs give information (mooring allowed, ferry, service point, etc.).

questions.stem: A square BLUE sign bearing a white letter “P” means:

choices.text: Mooring permitted
Mooring prohibited
Marina 1 km ahead

provenance.ref: RGP, annexe 5, panneau E.5 — stationnement autorisé (P)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Mooring (berthing) is permitted at this spot.

questions.stem: A red-bordered sign bearing a white arrow indicates:

choices.text: An obligation to follow the shown direction
A mere recommendation
A bathing area

provenance.ref: RGP, annexe 5, panneau B.1 — obligation (suivre la flèche)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: It is a mandatory sign: you must follow the direction (the side) shown by the arrow.

questions.stem: A sign bearing a number (e.g. “3.50 m”) generally indicates:

choices.text: A limit on headroom, width or depth
The distance to the next lock
The waterway's number

provenance.ref: RGP, annexe 5, section C — restriction (hauteur, largeur ou profondeur)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: A restriction: for example the available headroom, width or depth (series C signs). A maximum speed is instead a mandatory sign (B.6).

questions.stem: On a flowing river, when an up-bound and a down-bound vessel meet, which has priority?

choices.text: The down-bound vessel
The up-bound vessel
The larger of the two vessels

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-6 §2 — priorité de l'avalant
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The down-bound vessel (going with the current) has priority because it manoeuvres less easily; the up-bound vessel leaves it the way and the chosen side.

questions.stem: On still water, two power-driven vessels meet head-on. What do they do?

choices.text: Each alters to starboard
Each alters to port
They both stop

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-6 §1 — rencontre (chacun sur tribord)
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Each alters to starboard (its right) so they pass port to port.

questions.stem: On inland waters, on which side does a vessel normally overtake another?

choices.text: On the port (left) side of the overtaken vessel
Always on the starboard side
By passing very close to it

provenance.ref: RGP, art. A.4241-53-11 §1 — dépassement par bâbord
provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: As a rule on the port (left) side of the vessel being overtaken, once sure the manoeuvre is safe; the overtaking vessel keeps clear of the one overtaken.

questions.stem: At a bridge or weir opening bearing prohibition sign A.10, where is navigation permitted?

choices.text: Only in the space between the two panels making up the sign
Anywhere in the opening, except in the centre of the channel
Preferably between the two panels, but without prohibition elsewhere

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: When a bridge or weir opening bears the prohibition sign A.10, navigation is forbidden outside the space between the two panels making up that sign; it is therefore allowed only between those two panels.

questions.stem: An opening of a fixed bridge is marked by one or more red lights. What must you conclude?

choices.text: The opening is forbidden to navigation.
The opening is recommended as the preferred passage in both directions.
The opening is reserved for upbound boats only.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: When certain openings of fixed bridges are indicated by one or more red lights (or the general prohibition signal A.1), those openings are forbidden to navigation.

questions.stem: When a vessel holding a right of priority at locks approaches the lock lay-bys or is moored there, how must the other vessels behave?

choices.text: They must facilitate its passage as much as possible
They keep their turn according to order of arrival
They must immediately leave the lock lay-by

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: When vessels holding a right of priority at locks approach the lock lay-bys or are moored there, the other vessels must facilitate their passage as much as possible.

questions.stem: During normal navigation periods, what is the maximum duration for which the manager may exceptionally authorise a vessel to moor in lock lay-bys?

choices.text: Ten days
Three days
Thirty days

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The manager may exceptionally authorise mooring in lock lay-bys during normal navigation periods, provided this does not exceed a maximum of ten days.

questions.stem: Regarding the footbridges and other appurtenances of locks and dams, which rule applies?

choices.text: Standing and moving about on them is forbidden, unless they are arranged to serve as a public passage.
One may stand on them freely, including on movable bridges during operation.
Moving about on them is allowed provided one wears a personal flotation device.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: It is forbidden to stand and move about on the footbridges and other appurtenances of locks and dams, unless they are arranged to serve as a public passage; it is also forbidden to stand on movable bridges during operation.

questions.stem: Under what condition may an overtaking vessel begin to overtake?

choices.text: Only after making sure that the manoeuvre can be carried out without danger.
Only after receiving explicit permission from the vessel being overtaken.
As soon as the vessel being overtaken has reduced its speed.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text provides that overtaking is permitted only if the overtaking vessel has made sure that the manoeuvre can be carried out without danger. The responsibility to verify safety thus lies with the overtaking vessel before starting the manoeuvre.

questions.stem: On a stretch where the course to follow is imposed by signs, what obligation applies to upstream-bound vessels with regard to downstream-bound vessels?

choices.text: They must in no case impede downstream-bound vessels and must if necessary slow down or stop.
They have priority and downstream-bound vessels must keep clear to let them pass.
They must sound a prolonged blast before crossing the channel.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text provides that, in such stretches, upstream-bound vessels must in no case impede the progress of downstream-bound vessels; in particular, where they have to cross the channel, they must if necessary reduce speed or stop to let downstream-bound vessels complete their manoeuvre.

questions.stem: Except when overtaking or passing, what is the minimum distance a vessel may approach a pushed convoy carrying the dangerous-goods marking?

choices.text: No closer than 50 m.
No closer than 10 m.
No closer than 100 m.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text prohibits navigating, except when overtaking or passing, closer than 50 m to a vessel, side-by-side formation or pushed convoy carrying the marking prescribed for the carriage of dangerous goods.

questions.stem: What does the basic rule provide concerning anchors, cables or chains during navigation?

choices.text: In principle, it is prohibited to let them drag.
It is always permitted to let them drag in order to slow the vessel down.
It is mandatory to let them drag when drifting.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text lays down the principle that it is prohibited to let anchors, cables or chains drag. This prohibition has exceptions (manoeuvres, small movements, authorised drifting, etc.), but the basic rule is indeed the prohibition on letting them drag.

questions.stem: What obligation applies to high-speed craft with respect to other vessels?

choices.text: They must leave other vessels the space needed to keep their course and to manoeuvre, and may not require those vessels to keep out of their way.
They have right of way and other vessels must keep clear of their course.
They may require other vessels to reduce speed as they approach.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text states that high-speed craft must leave other vessels the space needed to keep their course and to manoeuvre, and that they may not require those vessels to keep out of their way. They therefore have no right of way and must themselves leave the necessary space.

questions.stem: How should a vessel that drifts downstream bow-pointed upstream with its engine running ahead be regarded?

choices.text: As a vessel proceeding upstream, not as drifting.
As a drifting vessel, and therefore in breach of the rules.
As a vessel proceeding downstream required to give way.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text in principle prohibits drifting, but specifies that vessels descending bow-pointed upstream with their engine running ahead are regarded as proceeding upstream, not as drifting.

questions.stem: Under what condition may a ferry begin to cross the inland waterway?

choices.text: Only after making sure the crossing can be made without danger and without forcing other vessels to make a sudden change in course or speed.
At any time, since ferries always have right of way over other vessels when crossing.
Only after sounding one prolonged blast to warn all approaching vessels.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text states that ferries may cross only after making sure that the movement of other vessels allows the crossing to be made without danger and without forcing those other vessels to make a sudden change in their course or speed.

questions.stem: What minimum distance must a vessel keep from the stern of a vessel showing the signals for mine-clearance operations?

choices.text: Not within 1,000 m of its stern.
Not within 100 m of its stern.
Not within 500 m of its stern.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text requires that vessels must not approach within 1,000 m of the stern of a vessel showing the signals for vessels engaged in mine-clearance. The minimum distance to be kept is therefore 1,000 m.

questions.stem: A boatmaster observes a risk of collision. What must he do?

choices.text: Sound a series of very short blasts.
Sound a single prolonged blast.
Increase speed to clear the channel as quickly as possible.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text states that any boatmaster who observes a risk of collision must sound a series of very short blasts. It also specifies that passing or overtaking is only allowed where the channel is unquestionably wide enough for simultaneous passage, and that vessels on courses excluding any risk of collision must not alter course or speed in a way that could create such a risk.

questions.stem: Regarding the vessel's speed, what obligation falls on the boatmaster?

choices.text: Ensure a speed compatible with the characteristics of the waters and structures, neither below the minimum nor above the maximum limits set.
Always maintain the maximum authorised speed to keep traffic flowing.
Freely set his speed, as no minimum limit may ever be imposed on him.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text requires the boatmaster to ensure that his vessel's speed is compatible with the characteristics of the inland waters and of the engineering structures; it may be neither below nor above the minimum or maximum speeds laid down by the special navigation police regulations.

questions.stem: A crew member temporarily determines the vessel's course and speed. What is his position with regard to the steering rules?

choices.text: He is also responsible, to that extent, for compliance with the requirements imposed on the boatmaster.
He bears no responsibility, the appointed boatmaster alone remaining liable.
He becomes solely responsible, fully relieving the boatmaster.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text provides that crew members and other persons on board who temporarily determine the vessel's course and speed are also responsible, to that extent, for compliance with the steering requirements imposed on the boatmaster.

questions.stem: While the vessel is under way, where must the boatmaster be?

choices.text: On board the vessel.
Ashore, provided he keeps the vessel in sight.
On board only when the vessel is actually moving.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text states that while under way the boatmaster must be on board. It adds that the operator of a motorised floating appliance must also be on board whenever the appliance is at work, even if it is not moving, these provisions not applying to floating equipment.

questions.stem: By day, what signal must a ferry not navigating freely display?

choices.text: A green balloon placed at a height of at least 5 m
A green cone, point downwards, placed at least 3 m high
A bright green light visible from all sides

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text provides that, by day, ferries not navigating freely carry a green balloon placed at a height of at least 5 m. The other answers confuse this daytime signal with night lights or a non-existent mark.

questions.stem: Unless otherwise prescribed, what characteristic must the prescribed signalling lights display?

choices.text: A continuous and uniform light
A regularly flashing light
A light whose colour changes depending on direction

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text states that, unless otherwise prescribed, the prescribed lights must show a continuous and uniform light. Options proposing a flashing or colour-changing light do not match this requirement.

questions.stem: Unless otherwise prescribed, what shape must the panels and flags required by the on-board signalling rules have?

choices.text: Rectangular
Triangular
Circular

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Unless otherwise prescribed, the required panels and flags must be rectangular; their colours must be neither faded nor soiled, and their dimensions sufficient to ensure good visibility.

questions.stem: Among the following visual means, which is NOT provided for a vessel in distress to call for help?

choices.text: A yellow flag hoisted at the masthead
Rockets or shells throwing red stars fired one at a time at short intervals
A light signal made up of the group ...---... (SOS) in Morse code

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text lists, among others, a flag waved in a circular motion, rockets throwing red stars, and a light signal made up of the SOS group in Morse code. It does not mention a yellow flag hoisted at the masthead, which is therefore not among the authorised distress signals.

questions.stem: For a signalling cylinder to be considered sufficiently visible, what minimum dimensions must it have?

choices.text: A height of at least 0.80 m and a diameter of at least 0.50 m
A height of at least 0.60 m and a diameter of at least 0.60 m
A height of at least 1.00 m and a diameter of at least 0.80 m

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text specifies that the good-visibility condition is met when cylinders have a height of at least 0.80 m and a diameter of at least 0.50 m. The other figures proposed relate to different shapes or do not appear in the article.

questions.stem: Under what condition is the use of lights or signals other than those provided for by the signalling rules permitted?

choices.text: For communication between vessels or between vessels and the shore, provided they cannot be confused with the lights or signals mentioned
Only at night and after authorisation from the authority responsible for navigation policing
For any use, as long as the vessel is moored

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The text generally forbids using lights or signals other than those mentioned, but permits, for communication between vessels or between a vessel and the shore, the use of other lights or signals provided they cannot be confused with those mentioned. The other answers add conditions not found in the text.

questions.stem: When a prescribed signalling light fails to work, what must the operator do?

choices.text: Replace it without delay with an emergency light
Stop navigating until the light is permanently repaired
Wait until the next port to report the failure, with no other measure

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: When a prescribed signalling light is not working, it must be replaced without delay by an emergency light; restoration of a light of the prescribed intensity must take place as quickly as possible.

questions.stem: What does the regulation say about the use of lights, searchlights, panels, flags or pennants on board?

choices.text: It is forbidden to use lights or objects liable to be confused with the regulatory lights and signals
Any advertising lighting is allowed provided it is switched off at night
Dazzling searchlights are permitted if they are aimed towards the bank

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: It is forbidden to use lights, searchlights, panels, flags or other objects liable to be confused with the regulatory lights or signals, to impair their visibility or to complicate their identification, as well as lights or searchlights producing dazzle that could endanger or hinder navigation.

questions.stem: You are operating a vessel that is not a small craft. What does the regulation require regarding the prescribed sound signals?

choices.text: It must, when needed, use the prescribed sound signals.
It may give these signals only when navigating at night.
It is exempt from giving any prescribed sound signal.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Every vessel, except small craft, must when needed use the sound signals set out in chapter III of annex 4. For such vessels the giving of these signals is an obligation, not a mere option.

questions.stem: Under what condition is the use of sound signals other than the prescribed ones permitted between vessels or between a vessel and the shore?

choices.text: Provided they cannot be confused with the prescribed signals.
Provided they are given only when approaching a lock.
Provided they are louder than the prescribed signals.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Using sound signals other than the prescribed ones is forbidden, except, for communication between vessels or between a vessel and the shore, provided they cannot be confused with the prescribed signals.

questions.stem: To call for help, what sound signal may a vessel in distress give?

choices.text: Bell peals or repeated prolonged blasts
A single short blast followed by a one-minute silence
Three short blasts given only once

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: A vessel in distress wishing to call for help may sound bell peals or repeated prolonged blasts; these signals replace or supplement the visual distress signals.

questions.stem: Where are the prohibition, mandatory, restriction, recommendation and information signs of the inland waterway, together with their meaning, defined?

choices.text: In annex 5.
In annex 7.
In annex 8.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: It is annex 5 that defines the prohibition, mandatory, restriction, recommendation and information signs, as well as the auxiliary signs of the inland waterway and their meaning; boatmasters must comply with them.

questions.stem: Where are the buoyage rules applicable on the inland waterway, upstream of the first obstacle to seagoing-vessel navigation, defined?

choices.text: In annex 8.
In annex 5.
In annex 4.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: It is annex 8 that defines the buoyage rules applying upstream of the first obstacle to seagoing-vessel navigation and that specifies under what conditions the various buoyage marks are used.

questions.stem: Regarding equipment, what obligation applies to vessels, except for small craft?

choices.text: Be equipped with a radiotelephone installation
Carry a navigation radar device as a requirement
Have an automatic identification system (AIS) in all circumstances

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: All vessels must carry a device enabling them to give sound signals; in addition, vessels, except for small craft, must be equipped with a radiotelephone installation.

questions.stem: In what case is the use of a navigation radar device mandatory for a vessel?

choices.text: For certain vessels or certain navigation situations determined by ministerial order
For all vessels without exception as soon as they navigate at night
Only for motorised small craft

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The use of a navigation radar device is required, for safety reasons, for certain vessels or in certain navigation situations determined by order of the minister responsible for transport.

questions.stem: At a berthing area marked by sign B.12, what obligation applies to all vessels?

choices.text: Connect to an operational shore-side electrical connection point to fully cover their electricity needs during the stay
Switch off all electrical equipment and remain completely powered down during the stay
Run their onboard generator to feed power into the shore network

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: At such areas, all vessels must connect to an operational shore-side electrical connection point so as to fully cover their electrical energy needs during the stay.

questions.stem: In a section where anchoring is generally prohibited by a special policing regulation, under what conditions may a vessel nonetheless anchor?

choices.text: Only in the areas indicated by sign E.6, and on the side of the waterway where that sign is placed
Anywhere in the section, provided the vessel is marked at night
Only with the prior written authorisation of the manager

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: In sections where anchoring is generally prohibited by a special policing regulation, vessels may anchor only in the areas indicated by the authorisation sign E.6, and only on the side of the waterway where that sign is placed.

questions.stem: For mooring or warping, which of the following is a vessel NOT permitted to use?

choices.text: A handrail or a metal ladder
A mooring bollard provided for that purpose
A mooring ring fitted on the quay

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: It is forbidden to use, for mooring or warping, trees, guardrails, posts, bollards (markers), columns, metal ladders, handrails, and any equipment not intended for mooring; a mooring bollard, by contrast, is equipment provided for that purpose.

questions.stem: At a berthing/parking area, absent any other instructions, how must vessels arrange themselves?

choices.text: Side by side starting from the bank, on the side of the waterway where the sign is placed
In a single row aligned along the middle of the channel
Rafted together starting from the bank opposite the sign

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Failing other instructions, vessels must berth side by side starting from the bank, on the side of the waterway where the sign is placed.

questions.stem: At a berthing area where a special-berth indication sign (type E.5.4 to E.5.15) is set up, who may berth there?

choices.text: Only the categories of vessel to which the sign applies, and only on the side of the waterway where the sign is placed.
All vessels, on both sides of the waterway, with no category restriction.
The categories of vessel covered by the sign, but on either side of the waterway indifferently.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The rules state that only the categories of vessel to which the sign applies may berth, and only on the side of the waterway where the sign is placed.

questions.stem: For a berthed vessel with no boatmaster aboard, who is responsible for setting up the required watch or supervision?

choices.text: The owner, operator or other person running the vessel
The authority in charge of navigation policing
The manager of the harbour or berthing area

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Where the vessel has no boatmaster, responsibility for putting this watch or supervision in place falls on the owner, operator or other person running the vessel.

questions.stem: What requirement must a vessel's loading meet with regard to visibility for steering?

choices.text: The load must not extend the zone of direct or indirect non-visibility for steering the vessel.
The load may reduce visibility as long as a lookout is posted at the bow of the vessel.
The load must never exceed the height of the wheelhouse, regardless of visibility.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The load on board must not extend the zone of direct or indirect non-visibility required to steer the vessel, under conditions set by ministerial order.

questions.stem: How must the master of a vessel respond to the specific orders given by the officials and agents in charge of navigation policing for the safety or good order of navigation?

choices.text: He complies with the specific orders given for the safety or good order of navigation.
He may refuse to comply if he considers his own course to have priority.
He is required to comply only on maritime waterways, never on inland waterways.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The master complies with the specific orders given by these officials and agents for the safety or good order of navigation.

questions.stem: What obligation does the master of a vessel have towards the officials and agents in charge of navigation policing who record offences?

choices.text: Give them the facilities needed to carry out their task of recording offences.
Leave the scene immediately so as not to hinder their intervention.
Provide assistance only if the agents first present him with a judicial warrant.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: The master must give these officials and agents the facilities needed to enable them to carry out their task of recording offences.

questions.stem: What must the master of a vessel do regarding the documents that must be kept on board?

choices.text: Produce them upon any request by the agents in charge of navigation policing.
Send them by post only, within eight days of the request.
Produce them only to agents holding a written requisition from a judge.

provenance.source: Règlement général de police de la navigation intérieure (RGP/RGPNI) — Code des transports, art. R. 4241-1 et s.
provenance.licence: Licence Ouverte / Open Licence 2.0 (Etalab) — French official act, freely reusable with attribution.

questions.explanation: Every master is required to produce the documents that must be on board upon any request by the agents in charge of navigation policing.
